MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(1): The paper reports no empirical comparisons against any prior automated scoring systems or recent baselines (only internal RF results on the single Gakken dataset), so it fails to situate performance relative to existing methods. Because there are no baseline runs reported, fairness of comparisons cannot be assessed and claims of practicality are unsupported by head-to-head evidence. Although related work is described, the manuscript does not acknowledge or empirically compare to even well-known hybrid feature-based scoring approaches (e.g., prior automated scoring work), leaving a major gap in evaluation. | B(2): The manuscript does not compare its Random Forest–based system against any appropriate baselines (no classical bag-of-words baselines, no recent neural/textual-entailment methods, and no prior automated short-answer scorers on the same data), so key comparative evidence is missing. Because there are no baseline runs reported, fairness of comparisons cannot be assessed (same data/metrics/conditions not demonstrated). Although the paper cites some prior work (e.g. older hybrid-feature AES literature), it fails to acknowledge or empirically situate its performance relative to contemporary competing approaches, limiting claims about practical advantage or novelty for operational assessment contexts.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper reports no empirical comparisons against any prior automated scoring systems or recent baselines (only internal RF results on the single Gakken dataset), so it fails to situate performance relative to existing methods. Because there are no baseline runs reported, fairness of comparisons cannot be assessed and claims of practicality are unsupported by head-to-head evidence. Although related work is described, the manuscript does not acknowledge or empirically compare to even well-known hybrid feature-based scoring approaches (e.g., prior automated scoring work), leaving a major gap in evaluation.

reviewer_b.justification: The manuscript does not compare its Random Forest–based system against any appropriate baselines (no classical bag-of-words baselines, no recent neural/textual-entailment methods, and no prior automated short-answer scorers on the same data), so key comparative evidence is missing. Because there are no baseline runs reported, fairness of comparisons cannot be assessed (same data/metrics/conditions not demonstrated). Although the paper cites some prior work (e.g. older hybrid-feature AES literature), it fails to acknowledge or empirically situate its performance relative to contemporary competing approaches, limiting claims about practical advantage or novelty for operational assessment contexts.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=2] A(2): The system combines well-known components (feature collation, cosine-based semantic similarity, and Random Forest predictors) applied to a specific Japanese exam dataset rather than introducing a new algorithmic idea or modeling paradigm. The problem (automated short-answer scoring) is well-studied and the paper does not compare to strong existing baselines or introduce non-trivial methodological extensions beyond prior hybrid feature approaches (e.g., earlier hybrid feature identification work). While the application to Gakken examinations and engineering details are useful for practice, the contribution is mostly an application/engineering effort that mixes established techniques rather than a genuinely novel methodological advance. | B(2): From an assessment-specialist perspective this paper applies well-known supervised methods (Random Forests) and standard feature types (surface keyword matches plus cosine semantic similarity) to a specific Japanese national-style short-answer dataset, which is an incremental engineering application rather than a new algorithmic advance. Prior hybrid-feature automated scoring work (e.g., the 1998 hybrid feature identification approach) already established the core idea of combining lexical and semantic features for score prediction, and the current system’s main novelty is its deployment context (Gakken examination) and pragmatic human-in-the-loop workflow rather than novel modeling techniques. The limited item set, lack of baseline comparisons, and the authors’ own framing of the work as a practical first-step further indicate modest novelty rather than a substantial conceptual contribution that would surprise experts.

reviewer_a.justification: The system combines well-known components (feature collation, cosine-based semantic similarity, and Random Forest predictors) applied to a specific Japanese exam dataset rather than introducing a new algorithmic idea or modeling paradigm. The problem (automated short-answer scoring) is well-studied and the paper does not compare to strong existing baselines or introduce non-trivial methodological extensions beyond prior hybrid feature approaches (e.g., earlier hybrid feature identification work). While the application to Gakken examinations and engineering details are useful for practice, the contribution is mostly an application/engineering effort that mixes established techniques rather than a genuinely novel methodological advance.

reviewer_b.justification: From an assessment-specialist perspective this paper applies well-known supervised methods (Random Forests) and standard feature types (surface keyword matches plus cosine semantic similarity) to a specific Japanese national-style short-answer dataset, which is an incremental engineering application rather than a new algorithmic advance. Prior hybrid-feature automated scoring work (e.g., the 1998 hybrid feature identification approach) already established the core idea of combining lexical and semantic features for score prediction, and the current system’s main novelty is its deployment context (Gakken examination) and pragmatic human-in-the-loop workflow rather than novel modeling techniques. The limited item set, lack of baseline comparisons, and the authors’ own framing of the work as a practical first-step further indicate modest novelty rather than a substantial conceptual contribution that would surprise experts.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is generally well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states the practical contribution early (a human-in-the-loop RF-based Japanese short-answer scoring system). Notation and methodological description appear consistent enough for reproduction (hyperparameters reported), though the summary does not indicate strong use of formal notation and some sections (e.g., feature/ablation presentation and figures/tables) seem under-described. Overall the writing quality is good and readable, with only minor presentation gaps around visual/illustrative material and explicit baseline descriptions. | B(4): The summary indicates the paper is well organized (methods, experiments, conclusion) and states the main contributions early (a practical RF-based Japanese short-answer scoring system that defers final judgment to humans). Notation and hyperparameters are reported and related work coverage is adequate, and the writing quality is described as good; however, some presentation gaps remain—e.g., no baselines were compared and discussion of limitations (semantic entailment, data imbalance) could be clearer in places—so minor clarity improvements would make it exceptional.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is generally well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and states the practical contribution early (a human-in-the-loop RF-based Japanese short-answer scoring system). Notation and methodological description appear consistent enough for reproduction (hyperparameters reported), though the summary does not indicate strong use of formal notation and some sections (e.g., feature/ablation presentation and figures/tables) seem under-described. Overall the writing quality is good and readable, with only minor presentation gaps around visual/illustrative material and explicit baseline descriptions.

reviewer_b.justification: The summary indicates the paper is well organized (methods, experiments, conclusion) and states the main contributions early (a practical RF-based Japanese short-answer scoring system that defers final judgment to humans). Notation and hyperparameters are reported and related work coverage is adequate, and the writing quality is described as good; however, some presentation gaps remain—e.g., no baselines were compared and discussion of limitations (semantic entailment, data imbalance) could be clearer in places—so minor clarity improvements would make it exceptional.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): This work targets an important, practical problem—scaling short-answer scoring for national-style written tests—and provides an applied, human-in-the-loop system that achieves useful within-one-point agreement (70–95%) on items not requiring deep semantic judgment. From a practitioner’s perspective the approach (Random Forests with surface and cosine-similarity features) is a sensible, implementable first step and could be immediately useful for exam vendors and raters. However, impact is constrained by evaluation on only eight items from a single 2015 trial dataset, lack of baseline comparisons, and admitted limitations handling deep semantic/entailment cases, which reduce confidence in broad generalization or a field-changing influence. | B(3): The paper tackles a practically important problem—scalable scoring support for Japanese short-answer items in national exam-style contexts—and presents a deployable human-in-the-loop system that could reduce rater workload by producing high-confidence candidate scores (70–95% within one point for items not requiring deep semantic judgment). From an assessment-practice viewpoint this is a useful, pragmatic contribution because it aligns with operational constraints and defers final judgments to humans. However, impact is constrained by evaluation on only eight items from a single nationwide trial, limited handling of deep semantic entailment, and lack of broader validation or baselines, so it is unlikely to fundamentally change research directions or general practice without further, larger-scale validation.

From a practitioner’s perspective the approach (Random Forests with surface and cosine-similarity features) is a sensible, implementable first step and could be immediately useful for exam vendors and raters. However, impact is constrained by evaluation on only eight items from a single 2015 trial dataset, lack of baseline comparisons, and admitted limitations handling deep semantic/entailment cases, which reduce confidence in broad generalization or a field-changing influence.

From an assessment-practice viewpoint this is a useful, pragmatic contribution because it aligns with operational constraints and defers final judgments to humans. However, impact is constrained by evaluation on only eight items from a single nationwide trial, limited handling of deep semantic entailment, and lack of broader validation or baselines, so it is unlikely to fundamentally change research directions or general practice without further, larger-scale validation.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper addresses an applied NLP/assessment problem that is relevant to top-tier venues (automated short-answer scoring), but based on the summary it reads as a practical system demonstration rather than a rigorous ML/NLP research contribution. Key weaknesses for this venue are the very small, narrow dataset (8 items from one trial exam), lack of baseline comparisons or ablations, and limited evaluation protocols (exact/±1 agreement only) which reduce generalizability and scientific impact. With stronger experimental rigor (broader data, baselines, calibration/error analysis, and reproducible code) this work would better match the expectations of a top conference. | B(3): The paper addresses an NLP/ML-relevant problem (automated short-answer scoring) and targets practical assessment workflows, which fits the venue’s topical scope. However, the contribution is largely an applied Random Forest system evaluated on a small, narrow dataset (8 items from a single national trial), with no comparison baselines and relatively simple metrics, limiting methodological novelty and generalizability expected at a top conference. The framing as a pragmatic human-in-the-loop tool and its attention to assessment constraints are strengths for an applied audience, but the limited empirical evidence and lack of deeper semantic modeling reduce its competitiveness for a premier NLP/ML venue.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an applied NLP/assessment problem that is relevant to top-tier venues (automated short-answer scoring), but based on the summary it reads as a practical system demonstration rather than a rigorous ML/NLP research contribution. Key weaknesses for this venue are the very small, narrow dataset (8 items from one trial exam), lack of baseline comparisons or ablations, and limited evaluation protocols (exact/±1 agreement only) which reduce generalizability and scientific impact. With stronger experimental rigor (broader data, baselines, calibration/error analysis, and reproducible code) this work would better match the expectations of a top conference.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses an NLP/ML-relevant problem (automated short-answer scoring) and targets practical assessment workflows, which fits the venue’s topical scope. However, the contribution is largely an applied Random Forest system evaluated on a small, narrow dataset (8 items from a single national trial), with no comparison baselines and relatively simple metrics, limiting methodological novelty and generalizability expected at a top conference. The framing as a pragmatic human-in-the-loop tool and its attention to assessment constraints are strengths for an applied audience, but the limited empirical evidence and lack of deeper semantic modeling reduce its competitiveness for a premier NLP/ML venue.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The experiments are adequate to show a practical, human-in-the-loop scoring aid on the provided 8-item Gakken trial set, and the paper acknowledges key failure modes (semantic entailment limits, data imbalance) and reports hyperparameters. However, there are important gaps: no comparisons to simple or strong baselines are reported, the dataset is small and imbalanced limiting generality, and systematic ablations/sensitivity analyses (e.g., contributions of individual feature groups beyond noting case-grammar was discarded) are lacking. Related work is discussed sufficiently for context, but reproducibility is only partial (no code or public dataset and limited experimental breadth), so overall the empirical support is reasonable but incomplete. | B(3): The paper provides a plausible, practically-oriented system and reports performance on real nationwide trial data, but evaluation is limited to eight social-studies items from a single administration, with no baseline comparisons and no ablation/sensitivity analyses to isolate feature contributions. The authors appropriately discuss limitations (semantic entailment gaps, data imbalance, many zero scores) which strengthens validity claims, yet reproducibility is only partial (hyperparameters given but code and broader dataset access not provided). From an assessment-practice viewpoint the experimental scope and depth are adequate as a first-step demonstration but insufficiently comprehensive to fully support broader operational claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are adequate to show a practical, human-in-the-loop scoring aid on the provided 8-item Gakken trial set, and the paper acknowledges key failure modes (semantic entailment limits, data imbalance) and reports hyperparameters. However, there are important gaps: no comparisons to simple or strong baselines are reported, the dataset is small and imbalanced limiting generality, and systematic ablations/sensitivity analyses (e.g., contributions of individual feature groups beyond noting case-grammar was discarded) are lacking. Related work is discussed sufficiently for context, but reproducibility is only partial (no code or public dataset and limited experimental breadth), so overall the empirical support is reasonable but incomplete.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides a plausible, practically-oriented system and reports performance on real nationwide trial data, but evaluation is limited to eight social-studies items from a single administration, with no baseline comparisons and no ablation/sensitivity analyses to isolate feature contributions. The authors appropriately discuss limitations (semantic entailment gaps, data imbalance, many zero scores) which strengthens validity claims, yet reproducibility is only partial (hyperparameters given but code and broader dataset access not provided). From an assessment-practice viewpoint the experimental scope and depth are adequate as a first-step demonstration but insufficiently comprehensive to fully support broader operational claims.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper states clear, modest claims and presents experimental results (70–95% within one point on 8 items) that partially support them, but the evaluation is limited: no comparison to simple or strong baselines, few items from a single 2015 trial, and no statistical tests or cross-validation to account for class imbalance and sampling variance. Methodologically the use of Random Forests with surface and cosine-similarity features is reasonable, but the absence of baseline/ablation studies and limited dataset raise concerns about generality and robustness. Thus the core conclusions are plausible but not fully substantiated by the reported experiments. | B(3): The paper states clear, modest claims and provides experimental evidence (Random Forests with surface and cosine-similarity features) showing 70–95% within-one-point agreement on eight Japanese short-answer items, and it candidly reports limitations (imbalanced data, semantic challenges). However, the experimental methodology is weak for strong technical claims: there are no baselines or statistical significance tests, the dataset is very small and item-limited, and class imbalance undermines reliability of the reported rates. There are therefore logical gaps in generalizability and comparative effectiveness, though the authors appropriately frame the system as a pragmatic, human-in-the-loop first step rather than a fully autonomous scorer.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear, modest claims and presents experimental results (70–95% within one point on 8 items) that partially support them, but the evaluation is limited: no comparison to simple or strong baselines, few items from a single 2015 trial, and no statistical tests or cross-validation to account for class imbalance and sampling variance. Methodologically the use of Random Forests with surface and cosine-similarity features is reasonable, but the absence of baseline/ablation studies and limited dataset raise concerns about generality and robustness. Thus the core conclusions are plausible but not fully substantiated by the reported experiments.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear, modest claims and provides experimental evidence (Random Forests with surface and cosine-similarity features) showing 70–95% within-one-point agreement on eight Japanese short-answer items, and it candidly reports limitations (imbalanced data, semantic challenges). However, the experimental methodology is weak for strong technical claims: there are no baselines or statistical significance tests, the dataset is very small and item-limited, and class imbalance undermines reliability of the reported rates. There are therefore logical gaps in generalizability and comparative effectiveness, though the authors appropriately frame the system as a pragmatic, human-in-the-loop first step rather than a fully autonomous scorer.

main_claims: They developed an automated Japanese short-answer scoring and support system for national center style written tests that generates candidate scores based on rubrics and lets human raters revise them.

The system uses Random Forests with a mix of superficial and semantic predictors (including cosine similarity) to predict scores for short-answer items, achieving agreement within one point with human ratings for 70–95% of items when high-level semantic judgment is not required.

The approach is positioned as a practical first-step method that combines keyword collation and semantic similarity, with the final judgment left to humans; it is applied to a Gakken nationwide trial examination (8 social-studies items).
_summary.methodology: Build a prediction model using Random Forests with predictors including degree of surface fit features and semantic (cosine) similarity between student answers, model answers, and test items; train on collected human-scored answers and use the RF ensemble (default 500 trees) to predict multi-level scores, presenting recommended scores and probabilities for human revision.
datasets: Gakken nation-wide trial examination (fiscal year 2015) — 8 test items across world history, Japanese history, and geography (human scored answer set used for training/evaluation)
metrics: exact match rate (prediction equals human rating)
probability that predicted score differs from human rating by at most one point
limitations: Recognizing textual entailment and deep semantic equivalence remains difficult; the system relies on keyword collation and cannot fully resolve complex semantic differences.
Insufficient and imbalanced human-scored training data (many zero scores) degrades machine learning performance for some items.
Case-grammar (agreement of case elements) provided almost no effective predictive power and was not used.
System is intended to defer final judgment to humans rather than fully automate scoring.
_summary.topic_area: NLP / Automated short-answer scoring and educational assessment

reviewer_a.name: Practical ML Methodologist
reviewer_a.background: Senior research engineer in applied NLP and educational measurement with experience building and evaluating production-grade automated scoring systems and interpretable ML pipelines.
traits: focuses on experimental design, reproducibility, and clear reporting of modeling choices and hyperparameters
expects thorough comparisons to simple and strong baselines, ablation studies, and analysis of error modes
values robust evaluation protocols including cross-validation, confidence calibration, and case-level diagnostics
notices implementation and deployment considerations such as runtime, class imbalance handling, and human-in-the-loop interfaces
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: I evaluate whether the experiments support the claims, demand clear baselines/ablations and reproducible details, and assess practical robustness and usability of the proposed system.

reviewer_b.name: Assessment & Education Impact Specialist
reviewer_b.background: Senior researcher at the intersection of NLP and educational assessment with experience designing national exams, scoring rubrics, and studying the real-world impact of automated scoring tools.
traits: examines novelty relative to prior automated scoring literature and suitability for operational assessment contexts
prioritizes alignment with assessment theory, rubric design, and the implications for validity, fairness, and rater workload
looks for evidence of generalizability across item types, languages, and stakes, and the human–AI collaboration workflow
notices practical constraints of large-scale testing (scalability, ease of integration, explainability for raters and stakeholders)
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: I judge the manuscript by its contribution to assessment practice and whether the approach meaningfully advances or safely integrates into real-world scoring workflows, including broader validity and fairness considerations.
